sentences.text: So when we came to the barracks we were told to get to the counting early morning and taken to the factory by SS women.
And there in the factory, we worked with a group of Polish girls who were also in Auschwitz before, and were supposed to build airplanes.
None of them ever went up in the air, believe me.
Not the way we worked.
And we were locked in.
And we worked 12, 14 hours, then we were taken back to the barrack.
And then we got dinner, which was again, a piece of bread and some soup, but a little more than, than in Auschwitz.

And unfortunately, I was with two sisters from Prague who one of them wanted to be-- well, she wanted to help herself a little more than she should have, and started to tell about us to the SS commandant of our group.
She even told on her own sister.
Not always true, but something every morning when he counted us, she reported to him.
She was put in charge by him, got a little more food, and we got a little beating every time, and even her sister.
So that was a very unpleasant, unfortunate time, because it's one of your own, which hurts even more.
No.
She's, she's still alive and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't-- I don't want to tell.
But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she didn't have a happy life afterwards, believe me.
And I got a lot of beatings.
And one of the SS women who guarded us in the factory was very, very kind to us.
And she told me that she had no idea when they called her up and told her to come to a certain building in the little town where she lived and take clothes, and I don't know how much and something.
And she had no idea that she was going to go to, to a camp and be an SS guard.
So she was nice and she was kind.
She gave us always, especially me, she gave me always a piece of an apple or a piece of bread or something.
And all of a sudden, one of the other SS women found out about it and denounced her, and they stripped her and put her in as a prisoner.
And she paid very dearly for being nice and trying to help, which made me very sad.
But there was nothing we could do.
And every day, the British were bombing that area.
And they threw down leaflets, and they were telling exactly what they're going to bomb.
And it was always at the same hours.
And our factory was on the hill, which was in the middle of that little town.
And on top of that hill was that factory.
And they had bombed constantly around us.
And one day they bombed either too early or too late, I don't know what it was.
But we were still outside the building.
They couldn't lock us in and go to the-- to the cellar.
So we were lined up against the wall of the building as they told us, and they were standing with us.
And we-- I looked up like this and I had one of SS girls next to me and she shouted to me, don't look up, they'll see you.
I said, well-- that's what they figured, that if we look up to the airplane who is God knows how high he will see us.
They had no idea.
They were scared, they were shaking, they were grabbing our hands as long as they were outside with us.
The moment they were inside that had stopped, of course.
But we loved it when we saw the planes coming, of course.
And one day we heard shooting far off, but still you could hear.
And within a couple of days it got closer and closer.
And one day, I-- oh, I forgot.
I'm sorry.
I couldn't anymore.
I was so weak and I felt so bad.
And I had, in Theresien, had encephalitis and knew how, how it is.
So I just told my girlfriend who was working with me, don't get scared.
I'm just going to drop, and they have to take me to the-- back to the barracks.
And there was one room where you could put somebody who was ill.
And they put me in there and there was one other girl.
And we knew when there was three who are sick, they send you back to-- from there to Auschwitz.
So we always, you know, tried to God forbid, not be three.
And I pretended-- and the doctor, who was one of our prisoners who knew exactly what I'm doing, came with this SS doctor because he wanted to see what it's like if somebody has encephalitis.
And I knew exactly how to react, of course.
And so I stayed there a few days and really regained a little bit of strength.
And then somebody else got hurt, so I got up and went back.
And a couple of days later at 2:00 or 3:00 in the morning, sometimes like this, they came and said we have to leave.
And that was when you could hear the-- it was the Russians who were close.
You could hear already the, the, you know, shooting.
So they took us on the-- down to train station, put us in cattle cars.
But we couldn't get through yet, so we had to stand on the station for three or four hours at least.
And during that time, the British bombed out that factory totally.
They knew we were out.
They always knew exactly-- wherever a camp was, they knew.
And if you look, they never bombed any, any prisoner camps.
And they knew we were out, and they bombed that factory, which we still heard them talk about it while we were on the station.
And that is when they took us to Mauthausen, which is near Linz in Austria, and it's quite a trip.
And we had to go through Czechoslovakia.
And we always had to stay sometimes all night, sometimes only a few hours, waiting till there is a room for us to go on.
And that night, it was pouring rain.
And all we had was-- each car had one little soldier who had-- who was on the outside of the car in his little enclosure.
And we had one who was very young, and very-- he had lost an eye at the front, and so he was sent back and given that kind of duty.
And it was pouring rain and we knew he was in his little hut.
And the girl, one girl in our group who was from the village where we stayed overnight on-- outside, and her parents, who were a mixed marriage, were living there.
And she begged us to help her to get up to the little window and-- so she can slide through, throw herself out on the other side and get away.
And we were scared that some others, you know, in other cars or so would see her.
But she said she doesn't care if they shoot her, but she, she has to try.
So we did.
Four of us put our hands together, said we are strong.
She wasn't that heavy anymore anyway.
And she made it.
She slid through, dropped on the side.
And after the war, I met her in Prague, and she said she went to a house, which was facing the train.
And I said, you know, we thought we saw a-- all of a sudden, a little light and then dark again.
Somebody had opened the door or whatever.
She said, yes.
They took her in, and the next morning they called her mother over.
And her mother took her home and she was hiding her till the end of the war.
She had gotten away with it.
We got to Mauthausen.
And Mauthausen is up the hill.
You have to walk quite a long time up to the camp.
So we were not in the best of shape anymore, but we managed.
And--
No shoes, no-- still the same thing.
No shoes.
My first shoes I got when I-- after the liberation.
Always that same little skimpy little dressing gown.
It was like voile, you know, like very thin material that you would have in summer, flowery, you know, with ruffles.
You know, one of those fancy things, sleeveless.
Any, all year.
Winter, summer, spring, makes no difference.
That was winter still.
I think March.
It was pretty cold.
But you know, I firmly believe that if I would today go outdoors in cold weather and snow without shoes, without underwear, with that little dress I would at least get a cold, if not pneumonia.
And we were going all winter long standing for hours without being allowed to move in the snow or in the cold.
Nobody got sick.
I mean, not this kind.
Not a sore throat, not a cold, not a--
I think God watched over us somehow.
I firmly believe it.
Something is not-- was not the normal way, you know?
Where you are starved, you are cold, you are-- and you still managed not to get really ill, which in normal lifetime wouldn't be possible.
And in Mauthausen, we walked up, row of fives.
And when we got to-- through the-- through the gate into the camp, we were going very slow, always only a few rows in the gate.
And then all of a sudden it stopped, and I think it was two or three rows ahead of me.
And we found out that they were gassing the last day and they had just closed.
So some of our girls still went in there.
And we went into barracks.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were-- There we had no-- we were just lying on the floor.
There was nothing.
And it just so happens that against the wall facing this way was a group of gypsies.
And I was in this row facing them.
And there was a little room, you know, to work.
And then behind me was again a row of girls facing-- or women, facing the other way.
That's how they put us.
And these gypsies went every day to work in the potato fields or somewhere.
Or the potato-- where they, they kept the potatoes.
They were not, not bringing them in.
They were-- and they stole.
They had clothes.
They had long skirts, like you know.
The-- they were dressed the way they brought them in, and they never lost their clothing.
And they had shoes and they had shawls--
And, and one gypsy who was lying facing me heard me talk to a friend who was lying next to me about my husband being from Olmutz, from that town.
And the moment she heard that, she came over to me.
And before, they never had anything to do with us.
And she said, you are from Olmutz?
I said no, I'm not, my husband.
She said, I'm from Olmutz.
And there is an amusement park there, and she was one of those hand reading gypsies who had a little booth there.
And from that moment on, that woman was helping me.
And she kept saying, now when you get back to Olmutz, you have to come.
You have to see me at the thing and your husband too.
And just because of that she was all of a sudden very nice to me.
And when they brought potatoes, she peeled the potatoes.
They ate them as is, raw, and gave me potato peels.
Which was fine, because all the good stuff really is in the peel.
I mean, it's not the best thing to eat, but at that time it was like the best dinner I ever had.
And it helped a lot.
And then we had to also stand every day outside, and it was still cold, for counting.
And in the meantime, they-- we had sort of an old broom.
We were supposed to sweep a little bit between the rows of the-- where we were lying.
So she said, OK, from now on, you don't go outside in the morning, you sweep.
Because it was much easier for me to do that than to stand in the cold for two or three hours.
So she really saved my life in a way, because I was at the end of my strength.
And the potato peels and the sweeping of the barrack instead of standing outside was another guardian angel.
And that's how I made it through till we heard the Americans are coming.
And the SS ran away and we were guarded by old men.
There was a-- I don't know what they called the group, but they were all, you know, older men in their 50s who didn't even know what they were doing with us.
They were scared of us, because they were outnumbered and they figured those women could do something.
Which we didn't, couldn't have the strength.
But they were scared of us, and the SS had run away.
And our barracks were surrounded-- I was in the lower one-- by woods.
And they were hiding in the woods there because they didn't want to be found by the Americans when they come, which they eventually did.
And I had a very lovely experience, because there was all of a sudden a GI standing at the gate instead of a German.
And I wanted to walk out to see how, you know, that is.
And he said, no, you can't.
We are here to guard you.
Not-- for your sake, because they are hiding.
Until they find all these guys, we can't let you out.
And he started to talk to me.
And then all of a sudden, he gave me a lipstick.
Now you imagine somebody who hasn't washed with soap for months and months and had a little bit of hair and was a skeleton needed a lipstick very badly, but it was so sweet.
So I took the lipstick and thanked him.
And then he started to talk to me more.
And I spoke English, so it was the lucky thing.
So then he said let me call the lieutenant, because he needs somebody who can still stand on his-- her feet and speak the language.
So he called an officer, who came with a jeep.
And said would I come with him as an interpreter?
And first to go to the village, because it was liberated, and try to find food and try to find clothing.
So I went with him.
And we came to-- we went from house to house.
Took all the potatoes, all the bread, all the shoes, all the dresses what-- that we could find, and loaded everything on the jeep.
And we came to one house where they had a little garden with salad, little, young salad, green salad.
Don't forget, that was May already.
And the lieutenant said, tell her to give you some, because that's very healthy that your people can eat.
So I said to the woman, we would like to have a few of the salads and whereupon she answered to me, she can't give me any, she needs them.
And you know, somewhere I found the strength and I found this thing.
I don't know how, but I took my hand and I hit her so that I turned around myself.
And I was down, not she.
And the lieutenant picked me up, put me in the jeep, and said to the driver, take all the salad she has there.
So he did.
He pulled out everything.
But when that woman who saw that, you know, where I come from, said she can't give me any because she needs it, that did it.
I mean, at that point, I lost it, and I just hit her.
And he laughed, and we went back to the camp.
And that's when I got my first pair of shoes.
And then he made us take from the SS barracks where the women were living.
They had all blue-white curtains and bedspreads and everything.
So he said take everything and see what you can do if you found somebody.
We go to a house, they should give us scissors and needle and thread.
And we made a makeshift skirt, you know, where we just pulled it with thread together.
And then we made a square, put-- cut out the head so we can put it over, and stitched it.
And we wore that for-- till all the way to Prague.
But I worked with him, and he was-- one day he said to me, what is the most important thing you would like?
And I said, I'd love a toothbrush.
And he looked at me, and said for heaven's sake, Helen, can't you make do with the old one for a few more days?
And I said no, because I haven't seen one in two years.
He couldn't believe it.
Soap, I said.
He gave us soap.
But he had no idea of, you know, of those things, that it's possible that somebody can live like this.

So whenever we asked for something which would be the normal thing for everybody to have, he thought we should make do, till I explained to him that all these things are not something that we have seen in a long, long time.
And one day that little young GI at the gate was there again.
And he called me and said, I have something for you.
And I said, what?
So he pulled out a ring.
And he hands me the ring and says, this should bring you luck that you get home and find your family.
I don't know where he got the ring from, but it was very sweet.
So that was--
And then when they told us they would take us home to wherever, you know, we come from or wherever we want to go.
And I had a young girl with me in my group who was married in Theresien.
She came from Westerbork, Holland, but she actually was from Germany.
Had gone to Holland to save herself, and of course, that didn't work.
So she came there and married a Czech young man in Theresien.
So she came home with us because she had no family, looking for her husband.
So she came and we were taken by bus by-- from the Americans. to the-- Czechoslovakia to-- was divided at that time, American and Russian.
So they took us to the little town where the Russians were already.
And they put us on the train and took us to Prague.
And all the way on the train where-- every little town, we had to stop, because people came, brought us food.
And I kept telling everybody, be careful, because you know the Czech people eat a lot of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] goose fat.
They brought bread with goose fat.
And this, on the-- our stomachs, absolutely impossible.
Because even when we were in Mauthausen, one of the ladies didn't listen and ate soup, which they meant well.
They took from whatever they found from the Germans left over.
It was peas and beans and-- what they ate, not our food-- and made sort of a one-pot thing.
And I told them, don't eat that, because we can't take it.
Our stomach can't take beans and peas.
And there was-- they found some bacon.
They had cut into it.
I said, for heaven's sake don't.
And that one woman didn't listen.
And she ate it and ate it and loved it, and then she died, same day.
Because she was just couldn't.
So when we went by train I said, dry bread, dry tomatoes-- potatoes, and anything that is not fat and not heavy we can eat.
But otherwise, you have to be very careful.
They meant very well.
They wanted to give us whatever they had saved, you know, under the counter during the war and hidden away, and with butter and with fat.
So we got to Prague and it was like 2:00 or 3:00 in the morning.
And well, my husband I didn't find anymore.
I went and looked, talked to hundreds of people and to the Red Cross and to the Jewish community.
And then I found out exactly what happened, where he was killed, when, and his brother.
And his parents and sister I had kept in Theresien.
When I left, I had the possibility, so I picked them up and they went home to Olmutz.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I found you know, some old friends in Prague.
And I was lucky because we had left some money with our lawyer who was Czech and not Jewish when we left.
So when I went there, to him, I at least had money and could-- You couldn't buy much that was any good.
I mean, even clothing, but still better than, than what we had.
And I wanted to get out of the Russian-- Prague was Russian occupied.
And it was dangerous, and they were very strange.
They-- we all had our camp here, you know.
I had Mauthausen here on my shirt.
But I went to a friend of my mother who had kept a watch and some rings and the things that she had on the last day when we went to, to go away.
And I got it, and I left and I walked.
And the first Russian soldier took it away from me.
They come and, you know, everything.
They had watches from here to here on every arm.
And I wanted to get out.
And I went into the American sector of Czecho-- of Czechoslovakia with a girl friend of mine.
And it used to be a very beautiful spot before the war, and it was still a very nice little town.
And they still had the Americans to occupy it, the very beautiful hotel in there.
And because I spoke English, I got a job with Red Cross.
And the director was a lovely lady.
And I worked with her, and I met officers.
And then the general gave a big dinner party.

And I was invited and met the American-- new American ambassador to Czechoslovakia, who couldn't go to Prague yet because they were fixing up some building for them to live, and his family wasn't there yet.
And so on that evening at the party, we were talking, and he asked me-- he heard about that I was in camp.
And he asked me what I'm going to do.
And I said, you know, I want to get out, because I have family in America, I know that, my father's brother and his wife.
And I have another aunt and uncle in Canada.
And they won't let me out now, because it was already very much-- it wasn't-- the communists hadn't taken over.
But in Prague, they had already their hands in everything.
And if you had money or if you had, you know, something valuable in real estate or so, they wouldn't let you out till you gave it to them.
And they couldn't take it away yet legally because they were not really legally, you know, allowed to do that.
So I said, I have to get out illegally.
I, I did it once.
I stayed too long.
I'm not going to do it again.
He said, I'll fix it for you.
And so he talked to the general that evening.
And he called me and said, I have a friend in Ravensburg, Germany.
I'm going to talk to him, and I think I can help you to get you out.
